In order to guarantee execution on your counterpart's side, there are several communication layers involved during the negotiation. The verbal, the choice of words, the paravirbal, how it's said, and the non-verbal, the body language. This is true of all conversations no matter the culture. Great negotiators understand how all three of these intertwine to affect their counterpart and weave them to maximize their effectiveness. At the same time, they are also able to recognize how their counterpart uses these factors to exert influence and reveal meaning. With this in mind, when asking your calibrated how questions, the great negotiator layers in the effectiveness of body language and tone of voice. The great negotiator may actually choose a tone of voice before they choose the words. Mastery is in tonality. The last thing you want is to ask your question and have your counterpart interpret it as an accusation. When asked correctly, calibrated how questions have the positive consequence of making your counterpart take a good hard look at your circumstances. How questions can cause someone to actually shift their perspective. The Black Swan group refers to this as forced empathy. By creating this dynamic of forced empathy, your how question works more as a notion than an actual question. What you say versus what they hear. It will force your counterpart to take your position into consideration and account for it. Think back to a time when you came up with a solution to a problem a team you were on was having. Did you feel a desire to ensure that your idea was successfully implemented?
